sentences.text: Yes.
The bank was totally destroyed.
It was a huge bank, with a very, very tall ceiling.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was stairs left, after this bombing.
There was no rail.
And there were huge boulders hanging on the iron wires, hanging from the ceiling.
We had sledgehammers.
I was the youngest in the group.
The kapo who was supervising us, he was a criminal.
He had a purple triangle.
His face-- he didn't say nothing.
Just-- no emotions.
He pick up one-- I think was 35.
The guy was in still fairly good shape.
He had the sledgehammer.
He told him to climb the stairs.
Many stairs-- slippery-- because it was-- this temperature, you know.
Every other step was just impossible.
The sledgehammer was very heavy.
The guy was doing frantic effort to be able to walk up and to reach the stair close to the big boulder hanging on the iron bar on the iron wire.
As he make a first movement by lifting the sledgehammer, he took a swing to hit the boulder.
He miss it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In this very moment, he was airborne.
He lost his balance.
He was still holding his hammer.
And he fell to a ground, crushing his skull on the-- on the stones, on the wires, on the irons.
He was dead.
The kapo pick up another guy-- I was the youngest-- and send him up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The guy was trembling.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was overcome by fear.
He saw the one dead to him, and the one mortally wounded, but he was walking.
He had reached the step with difficulty.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He swing the hammer.
He lost the hammer.
And he stood [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] motionless, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not knowing what to do, his hammer lying on the ground.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The kapo, enraged, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] told him to step down.
And in this very moment, I saw this unfolding tragedy.
I saw the guys older than me, trembling from cold and from fear.
I approached the kapo, and I said to him, I will do the job.
He looked at me, stunned.
But he smiled.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He said to me, you must be crazy.
I didn't intend even to ask you, and I wouldn't ask you to go up.
And you have volunteered to do it.
I say, yes.
He say, why?
I didn't say a word.
But I look around on the one dead, one wounded, and the one who was still in total fear.
And I didn't say a-- say a word.
He said to me, if you want to do it, do it.
But you shouldn't.
It was very strange, you know, to hear such a humane statement from a criminal.
He was a criminal.
What  he did, I don't know, but he had a purple triangle.
I was in still in good shape.
I was strong.
The few weeks in, uh, wherever, in Buchenwald, you know, I still was walking.
I was able to work.
I went up, and I look at the boulder looking for a split.
Because I knew hitting this boulder blind, this will never come down.
That the sledgehammer was bouncing like-- like a ball.
And I found a split.
And with all my power, I hit the split.
This was a connection between the iron wire and the-- and the boulder.
And there was no movement.
I did it a second time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stronger; and a third time.
And then, by the fourth time, the boulder-- tremendous size-- collapsed down.
And, without asking for another permission, there was another one hanging-- I did the same thing with another one.
I was trembling.
I-- my legs-- I felt I am on the verge of collapsing.
I think the kapo, he saw this.
Promptly, he sent two-- two guys, and they pick me up.
I went down, and he called me, and he said to me, he said to me-- amazing.
I cannot believe you were-- he told me, you have here 10 or 12 guys, 35, 30, and you were the youngest, and you did it.
You will get a reward.
A reward.
A reward, in Buchenwald?
From a kapo-- a criminal?
What kind of reward?
I didn't-- I didn't say a word.
He paused, and he said to me, you will get 10 potatoes.
I think this was a glorious day in my life, since I was in Buchenwald.
I was allowed to make a fire.
But I didn't have matches.
To get-- to get the matches, I have to approach the SS guard who almost killed the other guy.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
But I said to myself, I will do it.
What's going to happen?
He could holler.
He would say, I don't want to do it.
And he was eating, I remember, bread and sausage and soup and potatoes.
I look at this-- and I didn't see this for years.
I looked at the food, and I say to myself, I live here on a thin slice of bread, and this guy is all-- he's devouring food in quantities.
And I ask him, in a polite way, please give me a box of matches.
Which he did.
And he said to me, I saw you.
The way you did it, you do your job.
You deserve it.
And I bagged the potatoes.
I remember the envy of all the other inmates, looking at me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I will skip, because I don't want to go into more of the details.
On the way back, we went back to Buchenwald.
My brother was waiting for me, like for some kind of reason, I was late in this time.
And I said to him, I have a surprise for you.
He said to me, a surprise, in-- in Buchenwald?
That I got no surprises in Buchenwald.
No, I said, I got 10 potatoes.
He was-- he was happy.
And then we have a short dialogue.
How we will consume the potatoes.
You have to eat them at once, to stop this pain of hunger, of starvation.
Or we have to divide the potatoes in a couple-- in two or three days.
Then we reached the conclusion that we will eat the potatoes one per day.
One potato per day.
Well, now, we are reaching a moment [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where I got sick.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I knew that one of those days, I will be gone.
I-- I had fever.
I have occasional very acute influenza.
I felt I was dying.
There was only one way to ask for medical help, which was nonexistent in Buchenwald.
You are alive or dead.
This was the slogan by the SS commander of the camp.
Anyone ask for medical help was dealt with injection of carbolic acid into his heart.
The death was instantaneous.
I didn't know that.
It was April-- springtime.
The water was dripping from the-- from the roofs.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The trees were getting green.
There was a patch of blue sky.
For the first time, I look outside, and I saw trees being dressed in green leaves.
And then I reached the hospital.
There was a group of about 100 inmates looking for help.
A doctor appeared in the door.
He was maybe 35, with receding black hairs.
Caucasian type.
I couldn't ascertain his nationality.
When he saw the group, he said, today, no, no.
No reception.
The whole crowd start to move.
And then I heard a voice in German-- 'Du!' Which means 'You!' A group of about five, six inmates-- not including me-- darted from their place towards the doctor.
Then he said, not you.
You imbeciles-- 'idiots'-- whatever he said-- something.
And he pointed at me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I approached him.
He look at me.
His first question are, are you a Jew?
I couldn't lie.
I say, yes, I am a Jew.
He look at me, and I say-- and he didn't say a word.
And, in the- in the whim of the moment, I felt that my life is on a scale.
Then he said to me, I happen to dislike Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There is one reason I will help you-- I will try to save your life.
Because the way you look now to me, you are hours away from death.
You bear a striking resemblance between you and my brother.
And when I saw you for the-- for the first glimpse-- glimpse-- then I went back to the time I didn't see him for [? five ?] years.
But remember one thing.
You are not a Jew.
If somebody asks you-- a doctor-- you are a Dutch, you are a French, you are a German.
But you are not a Jew.
I went into a little room.
And he said to me, wait.
He left the room.
And there was a mirror in this room.
And I look into the mirror.
And I saw a mask of death.
It wasn't me.
I wasn't sure if it was my face.
I look behind me, thinking perhaps somebody else is standing behind me.
No.
It was my face.
He appeared after four or five minutes with a man, nurse.
He was a Dutch.
Tall guy-- about 6 foot.
Very warm.
I remember I got a bath, a white sheet, a white pillow-- which I didn't see for a year-- maybe more.
He brought me a bowl of soup-- milk and noodles.
There were packages arriving from Switzerland to Buchenwald, but not for the Jews.
I think the SS were taking care [INAUDIBLE] food.
But hospital had [? much ?] [? better ?] foods.
I recall eating the noodles in a slow portion, in a slow way, noodle by noodle, to prolong the divine taste of food I didn't taste for years.
And then my mind went to my brother, which was hungry and starving.
And I remember I couldn't swallow the rest of the soup-- which was unusual.
I was hungry.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So it was the spring.
The window was open during the day.
April was unfolding in majestic beauty outside the gate.
Because inside Buchenwald there was no greenery, no trees.
Only soil-- black, grey soil, covered with dispersed human ashes.
Because the Germans didn't know what to do with tons of ashes being accumulated for-- for seven years-- whatever it was.
Then, on the loudspeaker, there were coming orders in rapid-- rapid orders, in German.
[SPEAKING GERMAN], which meant 'All  the Jews to the gate.' 'All the Jews to the gate.' No mention of French, Dutch, Poles, Hungarian, Romanian-- Jews.
Because it was a big question.
And I paid a visit in Buchenwald in 1991.
I didn't find-- I found all nationalities.
But I didn't find gypsies-- Jews and gypsies.
I found American, English, Dutch, Norwegian, Spanish, Spaniards, Italian-- all nationalities.
No Jews. 30,000 Jews were in Buchenwald between the end of 1944 till April 9-- 10, 1945, the last evacuation.
To this very day, in the book of history-- and there are two excellent books-- and another book by Eugene Kogon, who died at age of 92-- and there isn't a mention about Jews.
For the German, as a [INAUDIBLE] flash, as a cover-up, the Jews were Poles, Norwegians, but no Jews.
But I am pointing out again that, in this hospital, the window open, I heard this order with my ears, to the best of my ability-- there was no hallucinating.
I was already in better shape.

And I saw a scene which I never forget in my life to the rest of my days-- thousands of people being herded by the SS, beaten, dragged, beaten-- beaten-- with German-- they used those German shepherds-- and being evacuated.

Between April 4th and April 10, the last day of evacuation, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] more than 20,000-- maybe 25,000 Jews-- were slaughtered-- in Buchenwald, on the road outside of Buchenwald, and on the death march-- death march from Buchenwald to Dachau.
My brother was among them.
This I learned later.

What's really painful to me, today, when I'm talking about these crucial moments of the liberation, when American armies-- the Third Army-- under General Patton, the Seventh Army, the First Army, the Sixth Armored division-- were three miles from Buchenwald.
There were patrols moving all around.
I didn't know then, but I knew later.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Under the-- their noses, the Germans succeeded to take out the people, the inmates, half-dead, to kill them, in the final demise of the Third Reich.
I'm not trying to accuse no one.
There is no hatred in my heart.
There is a lot of pain.
Especially the moment that I was liberated.
When I left the hospital, on April 11th, 1945, pleading with the doctor to stay another few hours.
Because the retreat was in progress from 11 o'clock.
It was after 11:00.
I read this in on April 22, 1985, the retreat of the German from Buchenwald.
He said no, you are in good shape.
You can walk.
In a few hours, maybe an hour, the American forces will be here.
I walked, and I saw the German SS by hundreds, armed, outside the barbed wire, retreating in disarray, frightened.
I was walking toward the main gate to look for my brother.
We gave a vow to each other that after the liberation, or at the liberation movement, we will search-- we'll look for each other among, you know, thousand, thousands of inmates.
I didn't found him.
Adjacent to the gates, hundreds of bodies were laying on the ground-- some in the last moment of their agony.
Voices-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- 'Listen, God of Israel!' [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Some stretching their hands for help, some dead, some covered in blankets, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dead.
I tried to search through the bodies, uncovering the blankets.
I saw faces-- all dead.
I didn't see my-- my brother.
I went back to barrack 62.
The barracks were deserted.
There was only one place.
There was also a hospital-- a small hospital in the little camp, called Krankenbau.
For some reason, later I learned that I went to the wrong hospital.
I went to the good hospital, because I left the small camp and went to the big camp.
But then I-- I realized that, a couple days, I was transferred from the small-- small camp to the big camp on the block 47 by the kapo who gave me the potatoes.
He transferred me from the small camp to the big camp.
Because I never could understood, how could I make the distance between the small camp and the big camp?
And there was a fence of barbed wire!
And just by a friend who refreshed my memory, I recall that I went to the hospital from the big camp.
When I went back to the barrack 62, there were dead bodies all over-- outside, inside.
I-- well, maybe it was around 12 o'clock-- I think I passed out.
I passed out, because I woke up, something like 1:00 or 1:00, 1:30.
I walk again through the-- through the gate-- again through the gate.
The camp was totally deserted.
I felt I am in ghost town.
I am a dead, walking among death.
This was my-- my main feeling.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Approximately [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] around 3 o'clock, 3:15 PM, I was close-- about 15 yards from the main gate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I saw a group of black soldiers-- Afro-American soldiers-- black.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tall, slender, beautiful giants.
I never saw a black soldier in my life.
I saw them in the movie, with Shirley Temple, being servants-- slaves.
But I never saw soldiers-- fighting men.
